h1Title: Selective IgA Deficiency: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
heroOverview: Selective IgA deficiency is the most common primary antibody immunodeficiency in Western populations, affecting approximately 1 in 300-500 individuals of European ancestry. It is defined by a serum IgA level below 7 mg/dL (or undetectable) with normal IgG and IgM. Most affected individuals remain asymptomatic throughout life and are identified incidentally; roughly 25-30% experience recurrent sinopulmonary infections, gastrointestinal disease, or allergic and autoimmune conditions. The risk of a severe transfusion reaction to IgA-containing blood products is a critical clinical consideration. No IgA replacement therapy exists; management focuses on prompt treatment of infections and surveillance for associated autoimmune disease.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1 in 300-500 in individuals of European descent; less common in East Asian (1:18,500) and African populations (Cunningham-Rundles 2001, PMID 11532460)
keyStats.demographics: Slightly more common in women than men; diagnosis often made in childhood or early adulthood; up to 70% of cases identified incidentally on pre-operative or blood-bank screening
keyStats.avgAge: Can present at any age; most symptomatic cases diagnosed in childhood during recurrent infection workup
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 2-3 million affected individuals in the United States alone; global prevalence varies substantially by ethnicity

definition:
Selective IgA deficiency (ICD-10: D80.2) is a primary immunodeficiency characterized by a serum IgA concentration below 7 mg/dL (or undetectable by standard assays) with normal serum IgG and IgM levels and normal functional antibody responses to protein and polysaccharide vaccines, in patients over 4 years of age, after exclusion of secondary causes. IgA is the predominant immunoglobulin at mucosal surfaces — the secretory dimer form (sIgA) protects the respiratory tract, gastrointestinal tract, urogenital tract, and oral cavity against pathogen attachment and colonization. Its absence impairs the first line of mucosal immunity but is often compensated by elevated secretory IgM, explaining why most individuals remain asymptomatic.

The condition exists on a spectrum: partial IgA deficiency (IgA 7-70 mg/dL) carries lower risk but similar associations. Full conversion from partial to complete deficiency occurs in up to 20% of cases. A related and more severe disorder, common variable immunodeficiency (CVID), shares genetic underpinnings and can evolve from selective IgA deficiency in 1.5-2% of patients over 10-20 years of follow-up. Both conditions are managed by allergy-immunology specialists, and patients with selective IgA deficiency require periodic IgG monitoring to detect this transition. The pathophysiology involves failure of B-cell maturation at the stage of IgA class-switch recombination, with several genetic loci implicated including the major histocompatibility complex region on chromosome 6p21.

typesClassification.description: Serum IgA undetectable or below 7 mg/dL with no associated infections, allergic, or autoimmune manifestations. Accounts for approximately 70-75% of all cases. Identified incidentally. No treatment required; annual monitoring for symptom development and IgG trends.

typesClassification.type: Symptomatic selective IgA deficiency with recurrent infections
typesClassification.description: Recurrent sinopulmonary infections — particularly otitis media, sinusitis, pneumonia — attributable to IgA deficiency. Requires aggressive antibiotic therapy and consideration of prophylactic antibiotics.

typesClassification.type: IgA deficiency with associated autoimmune or inflammatory disease
typesClassification.description: Celiac disease (10-fold increased risk), inflammatory bowel disease, rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus, and thyroid autoimmunity are all significantly more prevalent. The autoimmune disease is managed on its own terms alongside IgA deficiency.

Roughly 1.5-2% of patients with selective IgA deficiency develop falling IgG levels and impaired specific antibody responses over 10-20 years, meeting criteria for CVID. Annual IgG monitoring is mandatory to detect this transition early.

primarySymptoms: Recurrent bacterial sinusitis — episodes of purulent nasal discharge, facial pain, and congestion occurring three or more times per year, frequently caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, and Moraxella catarrhalis.
Recurrent otitis media in children — persistent middle ear fluid, conductive hearing loss, and repeated antibiotic courses not resolving between episodes.
Community-acquired pneumonia recurrence — two or more lobar pneumonias in a 12-month period, often with incomplete radiographic clearance, pointing to an underlying immunodeficiency.

Chronic diarrhea, malabsorption, and weight loss from concurrent celiac disease — occurring in approximately 10% of symptomatic IgA-deficient patients and requiring serology-adjusted diagnosis (anti-endomysial IgA is falsely negative; anti-tissue transglutaminase IgG or total IgG antibody panels must be used).
Giardia lamblia intestinal infection — IgA normally prevents Giardia attachment to intestinal epithelium; recurrent or persistent Giardia is a red flag for IgA deficiency.
Urticaria, allergic rhinitis, eczema, and asthma — atopic diseases are 2-3 times more prevalent in IgA-deficient individuals than in the general population.
Autoimmune manifestations — rheumatoid arthritis, thyroiditis, and systemic lupus are significantly over-represented; may precede or follow the IgA deficiency diagnosis.

Anaphylactic or severe transfusion reactions — up to 20-40% of IgA-deficient patients develop anti-IgA IgG or IgE antibodies; exposure to IgA-containing blood products (whole blood, FFP, platelets) can trigger severe anaphylaxis.
No symptoms in the majority — 70-75% of individuals with low IgA are discovered on routine screening with no attributable clinical problems.
earlyWarningSigns: Three or more bacterial upper respiratory infections per year in a child without anatomical cause (turbinate hypertrophy, adenoid hyperplasia) after age 2.
Persistent Giardia infection requiring multiple treatment courses without documented re-exposure — suggests mucosal IgA deficiency preventing parasite clearance.
Seronegative celiac disease presentation: villous atrophy on biopsy but negative anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA, prompting IgA quantitation.
Family history of IgA deficiency or CVID — first-degree relatives have 50-fold higher risk; baseline IgA measurement in siblings of confirmed cases is appropriate.
emergencySigns: Anaphylaxis following blood transfusion, intravenous immunoglobulin (if not IgA-depleted), or fresh frozen plasma in a patient with known or suspected IgA deficiency and circulating anti-IgA antibodies — treat with intramuscular epinephrine 0.3 mg immediately.
Rapidly worsening respiratory compromise in a patient with IgA deficiency receiving blood products — indicates anaphylaxis or severe immune complex reaction.
Signs of septic shock in a patient with known IgA deficiency during an acute infection — reduced mucosal protection accelerates bacterial dissemination.
New neurological symptoms during an episode of severe sinusitis in an IgA-deficient patient — cavernous sinus thrombosis or intracranial extension requires urgent imaging.

causes.cause: Genetic defects in B-cell IgA class-switch recombination
causes.description: The precise gene defect is unidentified in most cases. Several susceptibility loci have been mapped to the MHC region (chromosome 6p21), including HLA-DQ2/DQ8 haplotypes (shared with celiac disease). TNFRSF13B (TACI) mutations, also seen in CVID, are found in approximately 8% of selective IgA deficiency patients and cause impaired B-cell survival and isotype switching.

Phenytoin, carbamazepine, sulfasalazine, hydroxychloroquine, penicillamine, and gold therapy can suppress IgA production reversibly. Drug-induced cases typically recover when the offending agent is stopped. Always document current medications before labeling a case as primary.

Congenital rubella, cytomegalovirus, and Toxoplasma gondii infection during fetal B-cell development are associated with transient or permanent selective IgA deficiency through impaired lymphocyte maturation.

A positive family history is present in 20-25% of cases. Inheritance is polygenic rather than Mendelian in most families. First-degree relatives of an affected individual have a 50-fold higher risk of selective IgA deficiency or CVID than the general population.

The majority of selective IgA deficiency cases are sporadic. B-cell development reaches the IgA class-switch stage but arrests; the cells express surface IgA but fail to differentiate into IgA-secreting plasma cells. The specific signaling defect remains unknown in most sporadic cases.

riskFactors.description: Prevalence is approximately 1:300-500 in European-descent populations, 30-60 times more common than in East Asian or African populations. The MHC haplotype HLA-A1, B8, DR3 is strongly associated.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of IgA deficiency or CVID
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of patients have roughly 50-fold elevated risk. Affected families include members with selective IgA deficiency, partial IgA deficiency, and full CVID, suggesting a shared genetic background.

These HLA haplotypes predispose to both selective IgA deficiency and celiac disease, explaining their strong co-occurrence. HLA-DQ2 is present in approximately 95% of celiac patients and is over-represented in IgA deficiency.

riskFactors.factor: Use of specific anticonvulsants or immunosuppressants
riskFactors.description: Phenytoin, carbamazepine, sulfasalazine, and gold therapy are documented causes of acquired IgA suppression. Patients on these drugs with low IgA should have levels rechecked after discontinuation before a primary diagnosis is established.
Patients with celiac disease, rheumatoid arthritis, type 1 diabetes, and systemic lupus erythematosus have elevated prevalence of IgA deficiency, suggesting shared immunoregulatory defects.
Congenital infections interrupting fetal lymphocyte development, particularly CMV and rubella, are associated with IgA deficiency in the affected child.

affectedDemographics: Most prevalent in individuals of European descent: approximately 1:300-500; far less common in East Asian (1:18,500) and African populations.
Slight female predominance in most series (female-to-male ratio approximately 1.2-1.4:1).
Most symptomatic cases are diagnosed in childhood (recurrent infections) or young adulthood (celiac disease, autoimmune disease workup).
Asymptomatic cases are often diagnosed at blood donation or pre-surgical workup, skewing apparent demographics toward healthier adults.
diagnosisOverview:
Diagnosis begins with a quantitative serum immunoglobulin panel — IgA, IgG, and IgM — in any patient presenting with recurrent sinopulmonary infections, a family history of immunodeficiency, an unexpected transfusion reaction, or serologically 'silent' celiac disease. The diagnostic criterion is serum IgA below 7 mg/dL (or undetectable) with normal IgG and IgM in a patient over 4 years of age, because physiological IgA production is not mature before that age.

Once low IgA is confirmed, secondary causes must be excluded: review all current medications (phenytoin, carbamazepine, sulfasalazine), test for congenital infections in infants, and assess thyroid function and autoimmune markers. A specific antibody function assessment — vaccine titers to tetanus, diphtheria, and pneumococcal polysaccharides — determines whether functional humoral immunity is intact. Normal vaccine responses reassure against imminent progression to CVID.

Anti-IgA antibody testing is clinically important before any blood product transfusion: patients with anti-IgA antibodies require IgA-depleted blood products or washed red cells to prevent anaphylaxis. Screening for associated conditions should include anti-tissue transglutaminase IgG (not IgA) for celiac disease, ANA and anti-dsDNA for lupus, and thyroid peroxidase antibody for autoimmune thyroiditis. Annual monitoring of IgG levels and specific antibody responses allows early detection of conversion to CVID over follow-up years.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes the diagnosis: IgA below 7 mg/dL with normal IgG and IgM confirms selective IgA deficiency
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single blood draw measured by nephelometry or turbidimetry. Results available within 24-48 hours in most labs. Must be repeated on two separate occasions at least 4 weeks apart to confirm a persistent finding, per ESID guidelines.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses functional humoral immunity; low specific antibody titers in addition to low IgA suggest progression toward CVID
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw before and 4 weeks after pneumococcal polysaccharide and tetanus/diphtheria vaccines. A two-fold or greater rise in specific antibody titers confirms intact IgG antibody function.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies patients at risk for severe anaphylaxis with IgA-containing blood products; critical before any transfusion
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A specialized blood test performed at reference immunology laboratories. Not available everywhere; request via an immunologist. Results guide selection of blood products for future procedures.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for celiac disease in IgA-deficient patients; standard IgA-based celiac serology gives false-negative results in this population
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Must be combined with HLA-DQ2/DQ8 typing if result is borderline; duodenal biopsy confirms diagnosis when serology is positive.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates B-cell subpopulations and rules out more severe combined immunodeficiencies by quantifying CD19, CD20, IgA+ B cells, and T-cell subsets
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw processed within hours in a specialized lab. Selective IgA deficiency typically shows normal B-cell numbers with reduced or absent surface IgA+ B cells. Results in 3-5 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects falling IgG that signals progression from selective IgA deficiency to CVID over years of follow-up
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine blood draw once yearly. Downward trends in IgG below the lower limit of normal (550-700 mg/dL depending on lab) prompt specific antibody function testing and immunologist reassessment.

treatmentOverview:
No therapy replaces IgA itself: standard intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) preparations contain IgG as their predominant immunoglobulin and do not meaningfully raise serum or secretory IgA levels. Secretory IgA cannot be administered systemically. As a result, treatment is symptom- and complication-driven rather than disease-modifying.

For symptomatic patients with recurrent bacterial infections, evidence-based options from the European Society for Immunodeficiencies (ESID) 2022 guidelines include: aggressive diagnosis and prompt antibiotic therapy for acute infections (amoxicillin-clavulanate or doxycycline for community-acquired respiratory infections); low-dose prophylactic antibiotics (azithromycin 250 mg three times weekly or amoxicillin 250-500 mg daily) when sinopulmonary infections exceed 3-4 per year despite adequate vaccination; annual or biannual influenza vaccination; pneumococcal vaccination with 13-valent conjugate (PCV13) followed by 23-valent polysaccharide (PPSV23) at recommended intervals; and Hib and Tdap boosters per schedule.

Blood product safety is paramount: before any procedure, anti-IgA antibody status must be documented. Patients with demonstrable anti-IgA antibodies should receive IgA-depleted blood products or washed packed red blood cells. IgA-depleted IVIG preparations exist for patients with combined IgA deficiency and low IgG who require immunoglobulin replacement.

Associated conditions — celiac disease, autoimmune thyroiditis, IBD, and rheumatoid arthritis — are managed per their individual guidelines. If IgG levels fall and CVID develops, standard IVIG or subcutaneous immunoglobulin replacement becomes appropriate.

medicalTreatments.name: Prompt targeted antibiotic therapy for acute infections
medicalTreatments.description: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily for community-acquired sinusitis and otitis media; azithromycin or doxycycline for penicillin-allergic patients. Cultures and sensitivities guide therapy for recurrent or treatment-resistant infections.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolution of acute episode in 85-90% with guideline-appropriate antibiotic selection; clinical outcomes similar to immunocompetent patients when treated promptly

medicalTreatments.name: Prophylactic antibiotics for frequent sinopulmonary infections
medicalTreatments.description: Azithromycin 250 mg three times weekly or amoxicillin 250-500 mg daily during winter months or year-round when infections exceed 3-4 per year despite vaccination. Reduces infection frequency by 40-60% in antibody deficiency patients per ESID data.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 40-60% reduction in infection episodes in antibody deficiency cohorts; antibiotic resistance monitoring required with long-term use

medicalTreatments.name: Age-appropriate pneumococcal vaccination (PCV13 followed by PPSV23)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces the burden of Streptococcus pneumoniae infections, the most common serious bacterial pathogen in IgA-deficient patients. PCV13 given first, PPSV23 at least 8 weeks later per ACIP schedule.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Serotype-specific antibody responses normal in selective IgA deficiency (unlike CVID); 60-70% reduction in vaccine-covered pneumococcal disease

medicalTreatments.name: Annual influenza vaccination (inactivated)
medicalTreatments.description: Live attenuated influenza vaccine (LAIV) is generally avoided in immunodeficiency; inactivated injectable formulations are safe and effective. Annual dosing covers circulating strains.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Vaccine effectiveness 40-60% in matched seasons; reduces pneumonia-complicating influenza significantly

medicalTreatments.name: IgA-depleted IVIG (for patients who develop concurrent low IgG or CVID)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard IVIG contains IgA and may trigger anti-IgA anaphylaxis in sensitized patients. IgA-depleted IVIG formulations (Gammagard Liquid, Privigen in some markets) allow immunoglobulin replacement when IgG deficiency develops. Dosed at 400-600 mg/kg every 3-4 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduction of serious infections by 50-75% in CVID patients maintained at IgG trough above 700 mg/dL

alternativeTreatments.name: Nasal saline irrigation
alternativeTreatments.description: High-volume nasal saline (hypertonic 2-3% or isotonic) twice daily reduces bacterial colonization and mucosal inflammation in chronic sinusitis. Supported by randomized controlled trials for chronic rhinosinusitis (Harvey et al., BMJ 2007, PMID 17481090) and commonly used in IgA-deficient patients with sinus disease as an adjunct to antibiotics.

whySeeSpecialist: An allergist-immunologist is essential to confirm the diagnosis, document anti-IgA antibody status before any transfusion, monitor for CVID progression, manage associated autoimmune conditions, and design an individualized vaccination and antibiotic strategy. Pediatric immunology referral is indicated for any child with two or more serious infections per year, a family history of immunodeficiency, or failure to thrive.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a board-certified allergist-immunologist or clinical immunologist at a center affiliated with the Jeffrey Modell Foundation or the European Society for Immunodeficiencies (ESID) network. These centers perform quantitative immunoglobulin testing, specific antibody function assays, and anti-IgA antibody testing, and maintain longitudinal surveillance for CVID transition. For blood product management, ensure the treating hospital blood bank is aware of the IgA deficiency and anti-IgA status.
hospitalCriteria: Severe anaphylaxis triggered by blood products in a patient with known or suspected IgA deficiency
Recurrent pneumonia or complicated sinusitis requiring intravenous antibiotics
Diagnostic workup including bone marrow biopsy or specialized lymphocyte phenotyping when more severe immunodeficiency is suspected
Initiation of IVIG therapy when IgG deficiency or CVID develops
keyFacilities: Academic allergy and immunology clinics with primary immunodeficiency programs
Jeffrey Modell Foundation diagnostic and treatment centers
Pediatric immunology outpatient services for children with recurrent infections
Hospital blood banks with IgA-depleted blood product inventory
preventionStrategies: Ensure vaccinations are fully up to date — annual influenza (inactivated), PCV13, PPSV23, Hib, and Tdap — because intact IgG responses allow effective vaccine-acquired protection.
Document IgA deficiency status and anti-IgA antibody result in all medical records, including dental and surgical consultations, to prevent inadvertent IgA-containing blood product exposure.
Avoid drugs known to suppress IgA (phenytoin, carbamazepine, sulfasalazine) when safer alternatives are available, particularly in patients already at borderline levels.
Screen first-degree relatives of confirmed cases with a serum immunoglobulin panel, especially before general anesthesia or procedures requiring blood product support.
Use IgA-depleted blood products or washed red cells for all transfusions once anti-IgA antibody status is positive or unknown.
lifestyleModifications: Wash hands frequently and avoid crowded indoor environments during respiratory virus season to reduce infectious exposures.
Treat celiac disease with a strict, lifelong gluten-free diet if co-diagnosed — this is the single most impactful modification for the large subset with both conditions.
Inform all healthcare providers, pharmacists, and blood banks of the IgA deficiency diagnosis before any procedure.
Maintain a written medical alert card or wear a bracelet noting IgA deficiency and anti-IgA antibody status for emergency care settings.
Attend annual immunology follow-up even if asymptomatic — monitoring IgG trends detects early CVID transition before severe infections develop.

recommended: Strict gluten-free diet for patients with confirmed concurrent celiac disease — the most evidence-based dietary intervention in this population
Adequate protein and micronutrient intake to support immune cell production; no specific immunodeficiency diet otherwise required
Iron-rich foods if iron-deficiency anemia develops from celiac-related malabsorption
Calcium and vitamin D supplementation if celiac disease has caused metabolic bone disease
avoid: Gluten-containing foods (wheat, barley, rye) for patients with co-existent celiac disease
Foods associated with Giardia contamination (unfiltered water, unwashed produce) given elevated susceptibility to intestinal Giardia
Excessive alcohol which suppresses mucosal immunity and IgA production further
Unpasteurized dairy and juice products which carry elevated Campylobacter, Salmonella, and E. coli risk in immunocompromised individuals

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise is safe, beneficial, and encouraged for all patients with selective IgA deficiency. There are no exercise restrictions specific to IgA deficiency. Patients with concurrent autoimmune conditions (rheumatoid arthritis, lupus) should follow activity guidelines for those diseases. Good overall fitness supports mucosal immunity and reduces respiratory infection risk.
dailyManagement: Take prophylactic antibiotics consistently if prescribed; do not stop early due to feeling well — incomplete courses select for resistance.
Use nasal saline rinses once or twice daily during high-risk seasons (autumn-winter) to reduce sinus colonization.
Monitor for new autoimmune symptoms annually — joint swelling, unexplained fatigue, thyroid symptoms, skin rashes — and report to the immunologist.
Carry a medical identification document specifying IgA deficiency and anti-IgA antibody status at all times.
Complete all scheduled vaccinations; record dates and specific vaccine products for future reference.
Attend annual immunoglobulin monitoring blood test; do not skip follow-up even in asymptomatic years.
prognosis:
The outlook for selective IgA deficiency is excellent for the majority of affected individuals: 70-75% are asymptomatic and have a normal life expectancy without specific intervention. Among symptomatic patients, recurrent infections are manageable with appropriate vaccination and antibiotic strategies, and most do not cause permanent lung damage if treated promptly. The most clinically significant long-term risk is progression to CVID, which occurs in roughly 1.5-2% of patients over 10-20 years and substantially increases infectious and autoimmune morbidity; this progression is detected by serial IgG monitoring and managed with immunoglobulin replacement therapy.

The second key risk is anaphylaxis from IgA-containing blood products in sensitized patients — this is preventable with proper documentation and IgA-depleted product protocols. Autoimmune comorbidities, particularly celiac disease (10% prevalence) and thyroid disease, drive significant morbidity independently. Patients who maintain up-to-date vaccinations, avoid exposure to IgA-containing blood products, and receive annual immunological surveillance have outcomes essentially equivalent to the general population.
recoveryTimeline: Selective IgA deficiency is a permanent immunodeficiency in most primary cases; serum IgA rarely normalizes spontaneously (occurring in fewer than 5-10% of partial-deficiency cases and almost never in complete deficiency). Drug-induced cases typically recover IgA levels within 3-12 months of drug discontinuation. Infectious episodes resolve on standard timelines with appropriate antibiotics. Progression to CVID, if it occurs, typically evolves over 10-20 years.
complications: Progression to common variable immunodeficiency (CVID) in 1.5-2% of cases over decades — detected by falling IgG and impaired vaccine responses; requires immunoglobulin replacement.
Anaphylaxis from IgA-containing blood products in sensitized patients — preventable with anti-IgA testing and IgA-depleted product protocols; potentially fatal if unrecognized.
Celiac disease (approximately 10-fold increased risk) — if undiagnosed and untreated leads to malabsorption, iron and B12 deficiency, osteoporosis, and intestinal lymphoma.
Autoimmune thyroiditis and hypothyroidism — screen with TSH annually in symptomatic patients; managed with levothyroxine replacement.
Chronic sinusitis with nasal polyps and bronchiectasis from repeated untreated infections — annual spirometry and sinus CT in patients with frequent respiratory infections.
Giardia lamblia chronic infection with malabsorption — treat with metronidazole or tinidazole; recurrence warrants retreatment and consideration of prophylactic measures.

supportResources.name: Immune Deficiency Foundation (IDF)
supportResources.description: US-based patient advocacy organization providing educational materials, physician referral networks, and support groups for primary immunodeficiency including selective IgA deficiency.

supportResources.name: Jeffrey Modell Foundation
supportResources.description: Global network of diagnostic and treatment centers for primary immunodeficiencies; online symptom checker and patient registry resources.

supportResources.name: European Society for Immunodeficiencies (ESID)
supportResources.description: European clinical network with patient-facing resources, clinical guidelines, and specialist directories for primary immunodeficiency disorders.

confusedWithConditions.name: Common Variable Immunodeficiency
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: CVID involves low IgG plus low IgA and/or IgM, impaired specific antibody responses, and much higher rates of serious infection, organ inflammation, and malignancy than selective IgA deficiency. Selective IgA deficiency has normal IgG and normal vaccine responses. The two share genetic underpinnings and can be sequential diagnoses in the same patient.

confusedWithConditions.name: Allergic Rhinitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Allergic rhinitis is driven by IgE-mediated sensitization to environmental allergens and confirmed by positive allergy skin tests or specific IgE. IgA-deficient patients have elevated atopy rates, but their recurrent rhinosinusitis is predominantly bacterial and does not respond to antihistamines or intranasal steroids alone.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hereditary Angioedema
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hereditary angioedema is caused by C1-inhibitor deficiency and presents with bradykinin-mediated, non-pruritic angioedema unresponsive to antihistamines. IgA deficiency does not cause angioedema unless severe anaphylaxis occurs in anti-IgA-sensitized patients. Both are diagnosed by immunologist referral.

relatedConditions.name: Common Variable Immunodeficiency
relatedConditions.relevance: The most closely related primary immunodeficiency; shares genetic loci and can evolve from selective IgA deficiency over years of follow-up.

relatedConditions.name: Allergic Rhinitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Atopy including allergic rhinitis is 2-3 times more prevalent in IgA-deficient individuals; both managed by allergy-immunology.

relatedConditions.name: Asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: Asthma prevalence is elevated in selective IgA deficiency; recurrent respiratory infections may exacerbate asthma control.

relatedConditions.name: Drug Allergy
relatedConditions.relevance: Selective IgA-deficient patients have broader drug reactivity and elevated anaphylaxis risk to blood products and some medications; shared allergy-immunology management.

faqs.question: What is selective IgA deficiency?
faqs.answer: Selective IgA deficiency is the most common primary antibody immunodeficiency, defined by a serum IgA level below 7 mg/dL with normal IgG and IgM. Most affected individuals are asymptomatic; about 25-30% develop recurrent sinopulmonary infections, celiac disease, or autoimmune conditions. No IgA replacement therapy exists.

faqs.question: How common is selective IgA deficiency?
faqs.answer: It affects approximately 1 in 300-500 individuals of European ancestry, making it the most common primary antibody immunodeficiency. It is far rarer in East Asian populations (1:18,500). Roughly 70-75% of cases are identified incidentally and cause no symptoms.

faqs.question: What infections are common in selective IgA deficiency?
faqs.answer: Recurrent bacterial sinusitis, otitis media, and pneumonia caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, and Moraxella catarrhalis are the most common infections. Persistent Giardia intestinal infection is also characteristic. Viral and fungal infections are not significantly increased.

faqs.question: Can selective IgA deficiency cause anaphylaxis?
faqs.answer: Yes, in patients who develop anti-IgA antibodies. Exposure to IgA-containing blood products — whole blood, fresh frozen plasma, or standard IVIG — can trigger severe anaphylaxis. Anti-IgA antibody testing before any transfusion is essential, and IgA-depleted blood products must be used in sensitized patients.

faqs.question: Is selective IgA deficiency linked to celiac disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. Celiac disease is approximately 10-fold more prevalent in selective IgA deficiency. Standard celiac serology (anti-tTG IgA) gives false-negative results in IgA-deficient patients; anti-tTG IgG or total IgG panels must be used for screening. Diagnosis is confirmed by duodenal biopsy.

faqs.question: How is selective IgA deficiency diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires two separate serum measurements of IgA below 7 mg/dL with normal IgG and IgM in a patient over age 4, after excluding drug-induced causes. Specific antibody titers to pneumococcal and tetanus vaccines confirm functional IgG responses. Anti-IgA antibody testing is performed before any blood transfusion.

faqs.question: Can selective IgA deficiency be treated?
faqs.answer: No therapy replaces IgA directly. Treatment consists of prompt and prophylactic antibiotics for recurrent infections, up-to-date vaccinations, and management of associated autoimmune conditions. If IgG levels fall and CVID develops, IgA-depleted IVIG replacement becomes appropriate.

faqs.question: Does selective IgA deficiency get worse over time?
faqs.answer: In most patients it is stable throughout life. Approximately 1.5-2% of patients progress to common variable immunodeficiency (CVID) over 10-20 years, which involves falling IgG and impaired specific antibody responses. Annual IgG monitoring detects this transition before serious infections occur.

faqs.question: What is the difference between selective IgA deficiency and CVID?
faqs.answer: Selective IgA deficiency involves low IgA with normal IgG and normal vaccine responses. CVID involves low IgG plus low IgA and/or IgM with impaired vaccine responses and far higher rates of serious infections, organ inflammation, and lymphoma. Both share genetic risk factors and can be sequential in the same patient.

faqs.question: Can children have selective IgA deficiency?
faqs.answer: Yes. Selective IgA deficiency can present at any age, and IgA levels below 7 mg/dL in children over 4 years with recurrent infections should prompt immunological evaluation. A low IgA finding in children under 4 may reflect physiological immaturity rather than true deficiency and should be rechecked.

faqs.question: Does selective IgA deficiency affect COVID-19 risk?
faqs.answer: Selective IgA deficiency does not appear to dramatically increase COVID-19 severity in most asymptomatic patients. However, secretory IgA on mucosal surfaces plays a role in initial viral defense, and case reports of prolonged SARS-CoV-2 infection exist. COVID-19 vaccination is strongly recommended for all IgA-deficient patients.

faqs.question: Should IgA-deficient patients avoid certain vaccines?
faqs.answer: Live attenuated vaccines (nasal influenza spray, oral rotavirus) should generally be avoided in patients with known immunodeficiency. Inactivated vaccines including injectable influenza, PCV13, PPSV23, Tdap, and COVID-19 vaccines are safe and strongly recommended. Consult an immunologist before any live vaccine.

faqs.question: Is selective IgA deficiency hereditary?
faqs.answer: It has a genetic component without strict Mendelian inheritance. About 20-25% of patients have an affected first-degree relative. Several susceptibility loci in the MHC region (chromosome 6p21) and TNFRSF13B mutations are associated. First-degree relatives should be screened with a serum immunoglobulin panel.

faqs.question: What autoimmune diseases occur with selective IgA deficiency?
faqs.answer: Celiac disease (10-fold increased risk), rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus, autoimmune thyroiditis, type 1 diabetes, and inflammatory bowel disease are all significantly more prevalent. Annual autoimmune screening (ANA, TSH, tTG-IgG) is recommended for symptomatic patients.

faqs.question: Can selective IgA deficiency resolve on its own?
faqs.answer: Spontaneous normalization occurs in fewer than 5-10% of cases with partial IgA deficiency and is very rare in complete deficiency. Drug-induced IgA suppression typically reverses within 3-12 months of stopping the offending medication. Primary selective IgA deficiency is generally permanent.

faqs.question: What specialist treats selective IgA deficiency?
faqs.answer: Board-certified allergist-immunologists or clinical immunologists are the primary specialists. Referral to a center affiliated with the Immune Deficiency Foundation or Jeffrey Modell Foundation ensures access to quantitative immunoglobulin testing, anti-IgA antibody testing, and long-term surveillance for CVID transition.

simpleName: Selective IgA deficiency (low IgA immune deficiency)
metaTitle: Selective IgA Deficiency: Symptoms & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Selective IgA deficiency affects 1 in 300-500 people, causing recurrent infections and transfusion risk. Symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment reviewed.

Abolhassani H, Hammarstrom L, Cunningham-Rundles C. 2020. Current genetic landscape in common variable immune deficiency and selective IgA deficiency. J Intern Med 287(5):454-470.
Yel L. 2010. Selective IgA deficiency. J Clin Immunol 30(1):10-6.
Stiehm ER. 2015. The four most common pediatric immunodeficiencies. J Immunotoxicol 5(2):227-234.

Errante PR, Franco JM, Nobre FA, Ferreira JF, Kokron CM. 2012. Adverse reactions to intravenous immunoglobulin in patients with primary immunodeficiency diseases: a single-center prospective study. Clinics 67(12):1353-60.
European Society for Immunodeficiencies (ESID). 2022. ESID guidelines for the management of primary antibody deficiencies.
Immune Deficiency Foundation. Selective IgA Deficiency. IDF, Towson MD, 2023.
